info.title: getting started
info.text: In this demo we'll look at two different ways to connect your web page to an LLM (Large Language Model) via a REST API: one talks to OpenAI's servers in the cloud, the other talks to Ollama running locally on your own machine. Both use the same Fetch API pattern from the Fetch demo, the difference is where the model is running.

info.title: cloud vs local
info.text: When you send a request to OpenAI, your prompt travels over the internet to a data center somewhere, gets processed by a large model running on specialized hardware, and the response travels back. It's powerful but your data leaves your machine, it costs money per request, and it requires an internet connection.

info.title: local vs cloud
info.text: When you use Ollama, the model runs entirely on your own computer, your data never leaves, there's no per-request cost, and it works offline. The tradeoff is that local models are generally smaller and less capable than frontier cloud models. In this demo we'll integrate both.

info.title: global vars
info.text: First, we track five UI elements as global variables so they're accessible across functions. api starts as 'OpenAI' and switches to 'local-ollama' when the user changes the dropdown. This single string is all we need to decide which function to call when the user submits a prompt.

info.title: setup
info.text: The setup function builds the UI: a dropdown to choose between the two APIs, a password input for the OpenAI key (hidden text so it doesn't appear on screen), a text input for the prompt, a send button, and a paragraph for the output. A sharedStyles object keeps the layout tidy without repeating the same CSS properties on each element.

info.title: switchAPI
info.text: When the dropdown changes, switchAPI updates the api variable and hides or shows the API key input. The key field only makes sense for OpenAI, Ollama runs locally and requires no authentication, so there's nothing to enter.

info.title: postPrompt
info.text: When the button is clicked, postPrompt first shows a loading message, then calls either promptOpenAI or promptOllama based on the current api value. Both are async, so we await them. The whole thing is wrapped in a try/catch, if either request fails (wrong key, model not running, network error) we display a message and log the error rather than letting it crash silently.

info.title: promptOpenAI: headers
info.text: The OpenAI function starts by defining the request URL and headers. The Authorization header is how OpenAI verifies your identity, it expects a Bearer token which is your API key. Without a valid key the request will be rejected. You can get one from platform.openai.com/api-keys after creating an account.

info.title: promptOpenAI: body
info.text: The request body specifies which model to use, the user's input, and a temperature. Temperature controls how creative vs. focused the response is: 0 is fully deterministic, 1 is more "hallucinatory" or "creative". 0.4 is a reasonable middle ground for factual or task-based prompts. Check OpenAI's model list for available options.

info.title: promptOpenAI: fetch
info.text: We send the request with window.fetch() as a POST with the headers and the body serialized to a JSON string. Once the response arrives we parse it with .json() and log it, always worth doing the first time so you can see the full structure of what came back. The response text can be found inside json.output[0].content[0].text, which we set as the content of our output element.

info.title: promptOllama: local URL
info.text: The Ollama function is almost identical in structure, notice the URL: http://localhost:11434/api/chat. This is not a remote server, it's your own machine. localhost always refers to the computer you're currently on, and 11434 is the port Ollama listens on by default. The request never leaves your machine. To use this, you'll need to install Ollama and run ollama pull gemma4:e2b in your terminal first.

info.title: promptOllama: body
info.text: The Ollama body uses a slightly different shape than OpenAI's. The prompt goes inside a messages array with a role of 'user', this is the chat format used by most modern LLM APIs, where conversations are a list of messages with roles like 'user', 'assistant', and 'system'. Setting stream: false means we wait for the complete response rather than receiving it word-by-word.

info.title: promptOllama: response
info.text: The fetch call itself is identical to OpenAI's. But the response JSON has a different structure, the text lives at json.message.content rather than OpenAI's json.output[0].content[0].text. This is typical when working with different APIs: the HTTP mechanics are the same, but each provider chooses their own response shape, which is why reading the documentation matters.

info.title: which to use
info.text: There are tradeoffs between cloud-based and local models. Cloud APIs like OpenAI’s are often more capable, but can be costly (money, energy, etc) and offer less privacy. Local models (with Ollama or similar) give you more control and keep data private, but their performance is limited by model size and your hardware.
